description: Blueprint-to-architecture design with internal modes. mode_resolver picks the mode (design / stack / update / review / reconcile) and routes to a task graph; all five modes are implemented (design and stack from a blueprint; review / update / reconcile operate on an existing architecture and share an artifact resolver). design tasks: resolve blueprint input → detect existing architecture → prepare context → parse (incl. blueprint §9 Product Experience Direction + §19 Recommended Next Stages) → traceability map → clarify → solution strategy → goals/constraints → provisional tech stack (+ provisional tech assumptions + tech-stack selection handoff) → traditional-vs-AI boundary → skill/MCP decision → tech-stack/boundary coherence → C4 views → interface contracts → data contracts → security/trust boundaries → observability/audit → failure handling → testing/evaluation → ADRs → rule-pack review → draft (incl. Experience Architecture + Recommended Next Stages and Downstream Handoffs) → quality-gate self-check → final document. stack_tasks: resolve stack inputs → technology decision drivers → stack selection → architecture impact + update verdict → stack quality gate → final stack document (<topic-slug>-architecture-tech-stack.md). Pure-LLM transformation: every task is a reasoning step driven by a prompt file; there is no deterministic CLI/MCP backend. The manifest is valid as documentation for a prompt-driven agent and ready for a future runtime runner; if no runner exists, follow the task order manually. detect_existing_architecture is consumed by prepare_blueprint_context, solution_strategy, adrs, architecture_draft, and final_architecture_document so update behavior is respected end-to-end. v0.2.0 quality pass: the quality_gate_self_check now also enforces metadata consistency (counts match tables; A-N/ADR/Contents references resolve), hybrid-mode decision-review classification (decision source + review requirement), technology-specific validity (no enforcement claims the chosen tech does not provide), evaluator/probe availability policy, and the architecture-vs-implementation boundary (proposed namespaces, not file-by-file tasks). v0.3.0 hardening pass: a mandatory data-egress / external-model-use decision when external models are used; a document-wide residual invalid-claim scan (technology-inconsistent wording beyond the tech-stack section); explicit self-check skepticism using PASS / WARNING / FAIL (WARNING = directionally acceptable but needs cleanup, never a silent PASS over a known contradiction); a state-semantics consistency check (lifecycle states vs operational condition flags vs audit events resolve to the canonical §14 state machine); and an output detail budget so 'standard' stays a concise main body + appendices rather than a full dossier. v0.4.0 hardening pass: §17 security quality gates are rendered as a verification table (Security Gate | Required Implementation Evidence | Verification Method | Blocks Release?), never as ambiguous unchecked checkboxes; the residual invalid-claim scan now explicitly covers security-gate and checklist rows (not just prose), which is where leftover technology-inconsistent wording tends to hide; and the data-egress decision is expanded into a dedicated Data Egress / External Model Use table (content-leaves-boundary, which providers, redaction, logs-may-contain-source, domain-plugin-override) whenever external models are used. v0.5.0 hardening pass: high-impact decisions carry Decision Evidence (controlled provenance values) and are never labelled user-confirmed without an explicit confirmation source; raw source content is forbidden in logs by default for external-model systems (redaction + log-snapshot/provider-wrapper verification required); WARNING / PASS-with-warning gates are surfaced into the Executive Summary and Handoff, not just the §24 self-check; the standard output budget carries concrete targets; and architecture-stage build sequencing is capped at five high-level constraints (detailed task ordering belongs to the implementation-plan skill). v0.6.0 mode-split pass: the skill becomes one skill with internal modes. A mode_resolver entry task selects design / stack / update / review / reconcile (design and stack are fully specified; update reuses design's update machinery; review/reconcile are recognized but deferred). design mode now explicitly consumes the blueprint's §9 Product Experience Direction (producing an Experience Architecture section) and §19 Recommended Next Stages (producing a Recommended Next Stages and Downstream Handoffs section with tech-stack / ux-design / security-review / test-design handoffs), and keeps the tech stack provisional — with a Provisional Tech Assumptions table and a Tech-Stack Selection Handoff — whenever stack-mode selection is recommended. A separate stack_tasks graph selects the concrete technology stack against the architecture, ending with an explicit Architecture Update Required verdict, and writes <topic-slug>-architecture-tech-stack.md. The design output grows from 25 to 27 sections (adds Experience Architecture and Recommended Next Stages and Downstream Handoffs). v0.7.0 remaining-modes pass: review, update, and reconcile become fully implemented modes (no longer recognized-but-deferred). All three share an artifact resolver (prompts/resolve_artifacts.md, references/artifact-discovery-guide.md) that, when the user passes no filenames, discovers the most relevant prior skill/mode outputs by topic slug + candidate scoring, requires the architecture design, and emits a Resolved Input Artifacts table embedded in every output. review_tasks evaluate architecture quality without mutating it (10-point score breakdown + blocking/warning/polish classification + recommended next actions -> <topic-slug>-architecture-review.md). update_tasks apply already-accepted decisions (priority source: a tech-stack with Architecture Update Required = Yes, then accepted reconciliation, security-review, newer blueprint, explicit user decision; never ux-design directly) into an update note (<topic-slug>-architecture-update.md) without overwriting the design by default. reconcile_tasks compare the architecture with downstream feedback (primarily a ux-design Architecture Feedback section) to produce findings, a minimal patch plan, and an Architecture Update Required? verdict (<topic-slug>-architecture-reconciliation.md) without patching by default. No-silent-mutation rule: review never mutates, reconcile never patches by default, update never overwrites the design by default; bare `architecture` with an existing architecture defaults to review (safest).
